questions.text: Smooth muscles, cardiac muscles, and organs are regulated by which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The central nervous system consists of the brain and spinal cord. They are the main processors of the body. The central nervous system only analysis the information. It does not control the process of contoliing body functions. That is controlled by the periphery nervous system which consists of nerves, which relay information to the body. This is why the CNS is the incorrect option.

the parasympathetic nervous system is one of the two parts of the autonomic nervous sytem. The parasympathetic nervous system, relaxes the body after the events of flight or fight. It reduces heartbeat to normal levels, restores breathing, contracts pupils, etc. Although all these actions are controlled involuntarily, this is only half the part of the auntonimic nervous system.

The other actively working part of the autonomic nervous system, is the sympathetic nervous system. It works opposite to the parasympathetic nervous system. It prepares the body from fight or flight mode. Increases heartrate and breathing, dilated breathing, accelerates blood pressure, etc.

Autonomic nervous system and the somatic nervous system are part of the peripheral nervous system. The somatic nervous system controls all conscious activities, for example the movement of skeletal muscles. The autonomic nervous system allows for tight unconscious control of respiratory and heart rate and other critical systems of the body. Hence this will be the correct option.

questions.explanation: Smooth muscles, cardiac muscles and organs are controlled by the autonomic nervous system, as they donot require conscience control and are involuntary actions. Autonomic nervous system and the somatic nervous system are part of the peripheral nervous system. The somatic nervous system controls all conscious activities, for example the movement of skeletal muscles. The autonomic nervous system allows for tight unconscious control of respiratory and heart rate and other critical systems of the body. Hence option D will be the correct option.

questions.text: Which method from the following is an example of asexual reproduction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spores are cells of bacteria, fungi or plants which are created when an organism encounters harsh conditions. These spores have a thick cell wall which helps them withstand harsh conditions. They preserve the organism’s genetic material and resume multiplying once favourable conditions are available. The formation of these spores is known as sporulation.

Fission may be defined as the splitting of a unicellular organism into two or more separate daughter cells. There are two kinds of fission binary fission and multiple fission. Binary fission- It is the most common form of reproduction in prokaryotes and occurs in some single-celled eukaryotes.

Apomixis in flowering plants is defined as the asexual formation of a seed from the maternal tissues of the ovule, avoiding the processes of meiosis and fertilization, leading to embryo development. It is derived from the greek words, ‘apo’ and ‘mixis’ which means away from mixing.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: Since all of the above-mentioned options, include a single parent and no gamete formation is involved, hence all of the above options are types of asexual reproduction.

questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction is a type of reproduction that involves only one parent, and the offspring produced are genetically identical to the parent. This process does not involve the fusion of gametes (sex cells) and does not contribute to genetic diversity. Asexual reproduction occurs in various forms across different organisms, including bacteria, fungi, plants, and some animals. Methods of asexual reproduction are fission, sporulation, budding, vegetative propagation, artificial propagation, parthenogenesis and apomixis etc.

questions.text: Mumps and Measles are caused by which virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adenoviruses are medium-sized, nonenveloped viruses with an icosahedral nucleocapsid containing a double-stranded DNA genome. Their name derives from their initial isolation from human adenoids in 1953. These viruses typically cause mild cold- or flu-like illness.

Poxviridae is a family of double-stranded DNA viruses. Vertebrates and arthropods serve as natural hosts. questions.text: Manufacturing of Ammonia by the Haber's process is an?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Endothermic reaction: An endothermic reaction is a reaction in which heat is absorbed. This means that the energy of the products is less than the energy of the reactants. The Haber process is not an endothermic reaction because it releases heat when nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas react to form ammonia.

The Haber process is an exothermic reaction because it releases heat when nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas react to form ammonia. The heat released by the Haber process is used to heat the reaction mixture and to drive the reaction forward. The enthalpy change for the Haber's Process is -92.46 kJ. The negative sign signifies it is an "exothermic reaction".

Irreversible reaction: An irreversible reaction is a reaction that cannot be reversed. This means that the products of the reaction cannot be converted back into the reactants. The Haber process is not an irreversible reaction because the ammonia that is produced can be converted back into nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas.
Slow reaction: A slow reaction is a reaction that takes a long time to complete. The Haber process is not a slow reaction because it is typically completed in a few minutes.

questions.explanation: The manufacturing of ammonia by the Haber's process is an exothermic reaction. The Haber's process is a well-known industrial method for the synthesis of ammonia (NH3) from nitrogen (N2) and hydrogen (H2) gases. The balanced chemical equation for the reaction is: N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3 In this reaction, nitrogen and hydrogen gases react to produce ammonia gas. The forward reaction (from left to right) is exothermic, meaning it releases heat to the surroundings. As a result, the reaction is favored at lower temperatures. The Haber's process is carried out at high pressure and moderate temperature to achieve a good yield of ammonia while considering both the reaction rate and the equilibrium position. By removing the heat generated in the exothermic reaction, the process can be made more efficient and productive.

questions.text: Nitrates of which pair of elements gives different products on thermal decomposition?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nitrates of the elements sodium (Na) and potassium (K) do not give different products upon thermal decomposition. When nitrates of alkali metal are heated, they undergo thermal decomposition to produce corresponding metal nitrites (MNO2), and oxygen gas (O2). The general reaction for the thermal decomposition of metal nitrates can be represented as follows: Metal Nitrate (MNO3) → Metal Nitrite (MNO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) In the case of sodium nitrate (NaNO3) and potassium nitrate (KNO3), they would give their respective metal nitrides upon thermal decomposition: Sodium Nitrate (NaNO3) → Sodium Nitrite (NaNO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) Potassium Nitrate (KNO3) → Potassium Nitrite (KNO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) As you can see, the metal nitrites are formed from the thermal decomposition of sodium nitrate and potassium nitrate. Sodium nitrate produces sodium nitrite and potassium nitrate produces potassium nitrie. Therefore, the nitrates of sodium and potassium give same products upon thermal decomposition.

Nitrates of the elements magnesium (Mg) and calcium (Ca) do not give different products upon thermal decomposition. Both magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2) and calcium nitrate (Ca(NO3)2) undergo similar thermal decomposition reactions. The thermal decomposition of metal nitrates, including magnesium nitrate and calcium nitrate, leads to the formation of metal oxides, nitrogen dioxide gas (NO2), and oxygen gas (O2). The reactions can be represented as follows: Magnesium Nitrate (Mg(NO3)2) → Magnesium Oxide (MgO) + Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) Calcium Nitrate (Ca(NO3)2) → Calcium Oxide (CaO) + Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) As you can see, both magnesium nitrate and calcium nitrate produce similar metal oxides, which are magnesium oxide (MgO) and calcium oxide (CaO), respectively, along with nitrogen dioxide gas and oxygen gas during their thermal decomposition. Thus, in the case of magnesium and calcium nitrates, they give the same products upon thermal decomposition.

Nitrates of the elements lithium (Li) and sodium (Na) give different products upon thermal decomposition. Both lithium nitrate (LiNO3) and sodium nitrate (NaNO3) undergo thermal decomposition reactions. The thermal decomposition of alkali metal nitrates such as sodium nitrate, except lithium nitrate, leads to the formation of metal nitrites (MNO2), and oxygen gas (O2). The reaction can be represented as follows: Sodium Nitrate (NaNO3) → Sodium Nitrite (NaNO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) whereas, due to unique property of lithium, it gives the reaction as: Lithium Nitrate (LiNO3) → Lithium Oxide (Li2O) + Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) As you can see, lithium nitrate produces metal oxide, which are lithium oxide (Li2O) along with nitrogen dioxide gas and oxygen gas during their thermal decomposition like alkaline earth metals. and sodium nitrate decomposes to sodium nitrite and oxygen gas. Thus, in the case of lithium and sodium nitrates, they give the different products upon thermal decomposition.

Nitrates of the elements lithium (Li) and calcium (Ca) do give different products upon thermal decomposition. The thermal decomposition of lithium nitrate (LiNO3) and calcium nitrate (Ca(NO3)2) leads to the formation of different metal oxides and gases: Lithium Nitrate (LiNO3) → Lithium Oxide (Li2O) + Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) Calcium Nitrate (Ca(NO3)2) → Calcium Oxide (CaO) + Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) + Oxygen Gas (O2) As you can see, lithium nitrate produces lithium oxide (Li2O) as a product and calcium nitrate produces calcium oxide (CaO). Additionally, both reactions generate nitrogen dioxide gas and oxygen gas during their thermal decomposition. Therefore, in the case of lithium and calcium nitrates, they give same type of products upon thermal decomposition.

questions.explanation: The metal oxides formed from the thermal decomposition of sodium nitrate and lithium nitrate are different. Sodium nitrate produces sodium nitrite (NaNO2), while lithium nitrate produces lithium oxide (Li2O). Therefore, the nitrates of sodium and lithium give different products upon thermal decomposition.

questions.text: The strength of an acid depends on?

options.explanation: Ease of removal of a proton: The more readily an acid can donate a proton, the stronger it is. Acids with weak bonds between hydrogen and the rest of the molecule are more likely to lose the proton and, thus, are stronger acids.

This is often represented by the equation:Acid (AH) ⇌ H+ + Anion (A-)In this equation, the acid (AH) donates a proton (H+) to form the conjugate base or anion (A-). The strength of an acid is influenced by two main factors:Ease of removal of a proton: The more readily an acid can donate a proton, the stronger it is. Acids with weak bonds between hydrogen and the rest of the molecule are more likely to lose the proton and, thus, are stronger acids.Stability of the anion formed: After the acid donates its proton, the resulting anion (conjugate base) is formed. A stable anion is better able to handle the negative charge that results from gaining an electron. An unstable or highly reactive anion will make the acid weaker.Together, these factors play a crucial role in determining the strength of an acid. Acids with both a high tendency to release protons (ease of removal of a proton) and produce stable anions are considered strong acids. On the other hand, weak acids have a lower tendency to donate protons and/or form less stable anions.In summary, the strength of an acid depends on both the ease of removal of a proton and the stability of the anion formed after the proton donation.

Providing an external source of electricity: In a voltaic cell, the chemical reactions at the electrodes create an electric potential difference that drives the flow of electrons. If you provide an external source of electricity (e.g., a battery or power supply) and apply a potential in the opposite direction, it will force the cell to function in reverse, becoming a reverse galvanic cell. So, all of the options mentioned can convert a voltaic cell into a reverse galvanic cell.

questions.text: A chemical reaction has reached to a state of dynamic equilibrium at a certain temperature. Which of the following statements is incorrect?

options.text: The concentration of the reactants remains constant
options.explanation: In chemistry, a dynamic equilibrium exists once a reversible reaction ceases to change its ratio of reactants/products, but substances move between the chemicals at an equal rate, meaning there is no net change. It is a particular example of a system in a steady state. In thermodynamics, a closed system is in thermodynamic equilibrium when reactions occur at such rates that the composition of the mixture does not change with time. Reactions do in fact occur, sometimes vigorously, but to such an extent that changes in composition cannot be observed. Hence, in a dynamic equilibrium, the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant.

At chemical equilibrium, products are being produced and reactants are being consumed, it is just that at chemical equilibrium the rate at which the reactants (of the forward reaction) are consumed and products (of the forward reaction) are produced becomes equal to the rate of the backward reaction

options.explanation: At equilibrium, the rate of forward reaction is equal to rate of backward reaction. This is as per the definition of the equilibrium. When a reversible reaction is carried out in a closed vessel, a stage is reached when the forward and the backward reactions proceed with the same speed. This stage is known as chemical equilibrium.

Dynamic equilibrium does involve a reversible reaction, and the amount of each substance is stable once equilibrium is established. But during dynamic equilibrium, the reactions do not stop. Both the forward and reverse reactions continue at the same rate.

The acid-catalyzed conditions further enhance this electrophilicity by protonating the carbonyl oxygen, which increases the positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, making it more attractive to nucleophiles.

questions.text: The speed of photons in vacuum is _ than in liquid water?

options.explanation: The speed of photons in vacuum is greater than in liquid water. In a vacuum, such as outer space, photons travel at the speed of light, which is approximately 299,792,458 meters per second (or about 186,282 miles per second). In contrast, the speed of light in a material medium like liquid water is slightly slower than its speed in a vacuum due to interactions between the photons and the atoms or molecules of the medium. In water, the speed of light is approximately 225,000,000 meters per second (about 3/4 of its speed in a vacuum). This difference in speed is due to the refractive index of the medium, which quantifies how much light is slowed down when it passes through the material compared to a vacuum.

questions.text: A wavefront travels from air to the medium "Q". It makes an angle of 45˚ with the boundary in air and makes an angle of 30˚ with the boundary in the medium "Q". What is the refractive index of medium Q?

This option is correct.To calculate the refractive index of medium Q, we can use Snell's law, which relates the angles of incidence and refraction to the refractive indices of the two media involved.Snell's law states: n1 * sin(theta1) = n2 * sin(theta2)Where: n1 = refractive index of the first medium (in this case, air)theta1 = angle of incidence in the first medium n2 = refractive index of the second medium (in this case, medium Q)theta2 = angle of refraction in the second mediumGiven:The angle of incidence in the air (theta1) = 45˚The angle of refraction in medium Q (theta2) = 30˚Let's denote the refractive index of medium Q as n2.Applying Snell's law:n1 * sin(theta1) = n2 * sin(theta2)Substituting the known values:n1 * sin(45˚) = n2 * sin(30˚)Simplifying:sin(45˚) = n2 * sin(30˚)Using trigonometric values:1/√2 = n2 * (1/2)Rearranging and solving for n2:n2 = (1/√2) / (1/2)n2 = 2/√2n2 = √2Therefore, the refractive index of medium Q is equal to √2.

questions.text: X-rays cannot detect;
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Sugar is too low-density to block X-rays.
Blood pressure is a measure of the force of blood pushing against the walls of the arteries. X-rays cannot detect this force.
Cholesterol is a type of fat that is found in the blood. X-rays cannot detect this fat.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: All of these options are correct. X-rays cannot detect sugar, blood pressure, or cholesterol. These are all substances that are not dense enough to block X-rays. X-rays are best at detecting dense materials, such as bones, metal, and some types of cancer. X-rays are a type of electromagnetic radiation that can pass through most materials. However, the amount of X-rays that are blocked by a material depends on the density of the material. Dense materials, such as bones, block more X-rays than less dense materials, such as air. Sugar, blood pressure, and cholesterol are all relatively low-density substances. This means that they do not block X-rays very well. As a result, X-rays cannot be used to detect these substances.
